channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-14518] allow slow request processing to be removed from health check
item.description: If a request is not being processed in a timely manner, it will mark the service unhealthy, which can lead to STONITH. However, in some cases when the server is very heavily loaded, requests may take longer than at_max to be processed and this shouldn't cause the server to be killed, since that will slow down request processing even further and put extra load on the backup server(s), slowing down their processing and possibly causing them to fail in a similar manner.
item.summary: allow slow request processing to be removed from health check

status.description: A resolution has been taken, and it is awaiting verification by reporter. From here issues are either reopened, or are closed.

comments.comment: I think the last version of the patch that landed was a real fix for this issue.
Can this ticket be marked as resolved with the recent landing or is that just a diagnostic patch to help fix the actual issue.

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/53225/
Subject: LU-14518 ptlrpc: avoid server STONITH for slow request
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: bc927408f63bc4b64a81f9f25c95445005bb8f66

adilger , thanks for idea, I wasn't able to achieve that, first I've made that 0x511 _TIMEOUT fail_loc to fail on specified services only, so not all RPCs are blocked then I've tried to limit number of threads and several writes too but with no result. My last idea was to use NRS 'delay' policy which a bit artificial way to keep requests inside NRS, so health_check will trigger, I didn't try it yet.

I will try your approach locally and move this forward

I was able to provoke "UNHEALTHY" state by setting the OBD_FAIL_OFD_COMMITRW_DELAY journal commit delay in OFD to larger than at_max and then sending a large number of small writes in parallel. Something like the following, could be used for the test case (with extra stack_traps to restore modified parameters):

        local oss_threads=$(do_facet ost1 "$LCTL get_param  oss.OSS.ost_io.threads_max)
        local delay=100

        # delay for long enough to exceed old UNHEALTHY check, but allow
        # threads to complete RPCs occasionally to keep service healthy
        do_facet ost1 "$LCTL set_param fail_loc=0x1e1 fail_val=$((delay*3/2)) at_max=$delay"
        for ((i=0; i < oss_threads * 2; i++)); do
                dd if=/dev/urandom of=$DIR/$tfile.$i bs=4k count=1 &
        done

        # enough time for 2 sets of writes to be blocked for $fail_val
        local end=$((SECONDS + delay * 4))
        while ((SECONDS < end)); do
                local health=$((do_facet ost1 $LCTL get_param health_check)
                [[ "$health" == "healthy" ]] || error "OST not healthy: $health"
        done
        wait

"Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>" merged in patch https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/53451/
Subject: LU-14518 libcfs: print CFS_FAIL_CHECK() location
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set:
Commit: 1a9bd418463871d0748340c5f5ac659291a05cc1

"Andreas Dilger <adilger@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/53451
Subject: LU-14518 libcfs: print CFS_FAIL_CHECK() location
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 3f1231a9deb9cde5f2b81d8899d80359ff73dbe7

"Andreas Dilger <adilger@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/53225
Subject: LU-14518 ptlrpc: WIP avoid server STONITH for slow requests
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 515100fb45dda4587c9d60b73a44258c7aba5bdc

Another note about that health checking based on services, it stops whole node if failed, so all severs will fail over which looks as overreaction in some cases, e.g. thread is stuck waiting for some event like another server recovery or so, or even worse if storage becomes overloaded for a moment in which case server movement to another node will not help, since storage remains the same, that would cause constant ping-pong failovers. The only case when that is helpful - deadlock situation or any other when node requires reboot. Detecting that by single thread timeout is too aggressive
I am duplicating here my comment in review: we could consider history of timeouts, e.g. ost.OSS.ost_io.timeouts to decide about service health taking into account amount of timeouts per time unit
